1: bĕrōdak balʾăḏān ben-balʾăḏān melek bābel — Merodach-Baladan (Marduk-apla-iddina II), the Chaldean king of Babylon who twice ruled Babylon (722-710 BCE and briefly 703 BCE) and consistently opposed Assyrian power. His envoys with a letter and a gift (sĕpārîm ûminḥâ) come to Hezekiah because he has heard of his illness. The diplomatic context: Babylon was seeking anti-Assyrian alliances. Hezekiah's reception of these envoys becomes the seed of the Babylonian exile prophesied in vv.5-7.
2: wayyiśmaḥ ʿălêhem Ḥizqiyyāhû — "Hezekiah was pleased/glad with them" — the dangerous delight. wayyarʾēm ʾet-bêt nĕkōtôh — "showed them his house of treasures" (nĕkōt, H5238, spice/treasure; bêt nĕkōt, the treasury). ʾet-hakkesef wĕʾet-hazzāhāb — silver, gold, spices, fine oil, the entire armory, and everything in the storehouses — Hezekiah shows everything. The comprehensiveness is repeated: lōʾ-hāyâ dābār ʾăšer lōʾ-herʾām — "there was nothing that he did not show them."
3: Isaiah's opening question: mā ʾāmĕrû hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh ûmēʾayin yāḇōʾû ʾēleykā — "what did these men say, and where did they come from?" Hezekiah answers proudly: mēʾereṣ rĕḥôqâ bāʾû ʾēlay mibābel — "from a distant land they came to me, from Babylon." The word rĕḥôqâ (distant) is the geographical marker that anticipates the later exile — what came from afar will take everything there.
4: mah rāʾû bĕbêtekā — "what did they see in your house?" — Isaiah presses. Hezekiah answers with complete candor: kōl ʾăšer bĕbêtî rāʾû — "everything in my house they saw." lōʾ-hāyâ dābār ʾăšer lōʾ-herʾîtîm bĕʾôṣĕrōtāy — "there was nothing in my treasuries that I did not show them." The repetition of "nothing not shown" from v.2 and v.4 frames the indictment — Hezekiah has confirmed his own guilt.
5: šĕmaʿ dĕbar Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt — "hear the word of YHWH of hosts" — the prophetic formula that introduces a hard word. The shift from conversation to oracle marks a change in register. Isaiah's words become the voice of YHWH, not his own assessment. The oracle will address the consequences of Hezekiah's hospitality to Babylon's representative.
6: hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm — "behold, days are coming" — the prophetic future formula. wĕnuśśāʾ kol-ʾăšer bĕbêtekā — "everything in your house will be carried off (nāśāʾ)" — the same verb as the Babylonian exile deportations. wĕʾăšer ʾāṣĕrû ʾăḇōteykā ʿad-hayyôm hazzeh bābel yūbāʾû — "what your fathers stored up until this day will be brought to Babylon." The treasure that Hezekiah showed becomes the treasure that will be removed — the showing was already the beginning of the losing.
7: ûmibānêkā ʾăšer yēṣĕʾû mimměkā — "and of your sons who will come from you" — Hezekiah's descendants will be taken. wĕhāyû sārîsîm bĕhêkal melek bābel — "they will be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon" (sārîs, H5631, eunuch/court official). This specific prophecy is regarded as fulfilled in the Babylonian exile — Israelite youths of the royal line serving in Babylonian courts (cf. Dan 1:1-7 where Daniel and his companions are exactly this). The use of sārîs may refer to court officials generally or specifically to castration.
8: wayyōʾmer Ḥizqiyyāhû ʾel-Yĕšaʿyāhû ṭôḇ dĕbar-Yhwh ʾăšer dibbartā — "Hezekiah said to Isaiah, 'The word of YHWH that you have spoken is good.'" The response is theologically proper — accepting the divine word — but the following clause reveals its limitation: kî yihyeh šālôm weʾĕmet bĕyāmay — "for there will be peace and truth in my days." Hezekiah is content because the disaster will not come in his lifetime. The commentary tradition has long noted the moral ambiguity: is this faith (accepting YHWH's sovereignty) or selfishness (relief that others will bear it)?
